my_sentence: I can bring my family to shopping mall and buy some clothes for us.
correction: I can take my family to the shopping mall and buy some clothes for us.
explanation: "Shopping mall" is a countable noun and needs an article: "the shopping mall." Also, "take" (not "bring") is used for moving people away from the speaker's current place. (assistant note)
correct_examples: I'll take my family to the shopping mall this weekend.
She took her kids to the park.
context: Weekend plans to go shopping with family.

my_sentence: I think for some places maybe they are not closure, but for some they are closure.
correction: For some places they are not close, but for others, they are close.
explanation: When contrasting two groups, the second group is "others," not "some" again: "for some..., for others...". Also the adjective is "close," not "closure" (a noun) or "closer" (comparative, which needs a comparison).
correct_examples: Some malls are far away, but others are close.
Some people like cities; others prefer the countryside.
context: Whether shopping malls are near apartment complexes in China.

my_sentence: In my city we have many shopping malls so you can go one of them very quickly.
correction: You can go to one of them very quickly.
explanation: "Go" needs the preposition "to" before a destination: "go to one of them," not "go one of them."
correct_examples: You can go to one of the malls in ten minutes.
Let's go to the nearest supermarket.
context: Talking about how many shopping malls his city has.

my_sentence: You can go to one of them very quickly. Very quick.
correction: Very quickly.
explanation: To modify the verb "go," use the adverb "quickly," not the adjective "quick."
correct_examples: You can get there very quickly.
She finished the task very quickly.
context: Same conversation about reaching nearby malls.

my_sentence: They usually... because they provide pretty good, convenient and...
correction: They provide pretty good products and a nice environment.
explanation: After "provide" you need a noun, not bare adjectives. "Pretty good" and "convenient" must describe something: "pretty good products," "a convenient service."
correct_examples: Shopping malls provide good products and a comfortable environment.
The store provides convenient delivery services.
context: Why shopping malls are not cheaper.

my_sentence: I checked their clothes. I think most of them are cotton.
correction: Most of them are made of cotton.
explanation: To describe material, use "be made of + material." Saying "they are cotton" is possible informally, but the teacher's point is the standard collocation "made of cotton."
correct_examples: Most of their T-shirts are made of cotton.
This sweater is made of wool.
context: Discussing Uniqlo clothing fabric.

my_sentence: Actually for me I don't care about they made from made of cotton or other things.
correction: I don't care whether they're made of cotton or something else.
explanation: "Care about" cannot be followed directly by a clause like "they made from cotton." Use "care whether/if + clause." Also "other things" is more natural as "something else" here. (assistant note)
correct_examples: I don't care whether it's made of cotton or polyester.
She doesn't care if the shirt is expensive or cheap.
context: Saying fabric type doesn't matter to him.

my_sentence: Being a detective. can get... challenging and exciting.
correction: Being a detective can be challenging and exciting.
explanation: The teacher pointed out "you're cutting your sentences" — the idea was delivered in fragments. Plan the full sentence and deliver subject + verb + complement in one flow.
correct_examples: Being a detective can be challenging and exciting.
Working as a detective is meaningful because you help people.
context: Starting to describe the pros of being a detective.

my_sentence: Being a detective can give you some opportunities to solve challenging cases and helping people.
correction: Being a detective can give you opportunities to solve challenging cases and help people.
explanation: Items joined by "and" after "to" should stay parallel: "to solve ... and (to) help ...," not a mix of infinitive and gerund. (assistant note)
correct_examples: The job gives you chances to travel and meet new people.
She wants to study hard and pass the exam.
context: Pros of being a detective.

my_sentence: Killing and murdering case.
correction: Murder cases.
explanation: Use the noun "murder" as a modifier: "murder cases," not "murdering cases" or "killing cases." The teacher corrected this again later in the written exercise ("such as murdering" → "such as murder").
correct_examples: Detectives investigate murder cases.
The police solved a murder case last month.
context: Types of challenging cases detectives handle.

my_sentence: Robbing, rob cases. Steal case.
correction: Robbery cases / burglary cases.
explanation: The crime nouns are "robbery" and "burglary": "robbery cases," not "rob cases" or "robbing cases." "Steal" is a verb; the noun is "theft."
correct_examples: The detective handled several robbery cases.
Burglary cases increase during the holidays.
context: Types of challenging cases detectives handle.

my_sentence: Maybe during that time when they married, they love each other.
correction: At first they loved each other, but as time went by they didn't love each other anymore.
explanation: The story is in the past, so use past tense: "they loved each other," "when they got married." Also "when time goes out" is not English — the idiom is "as time goes by / went by."
correct_examples: When they got married, they loved each other.
As time went by, they grew apart.
context: Discussing why married couples cheat or divorce.

my_sentence: Two or three years later they find they can't understand it. Behavioral or ideal.
correction: They find they can't understand each other's behaviors anymore.
explanation: Use the possessive "each other's" before the noun: "each other's behaviors." The teacher supplied "each other's behaviors anymore."
correct_examples: They couldn't accept each other's habits anymore.
We should respect each other's opinions.
context: Why couples stop loving each other.

my_sentence: Being a detective can let a person have different feelings... Let.
correction: Being a detective lets you have a different life.
explanation: A gerund subject ("Being a detective") is singular, so the present-tense verb takes -s: "lets you," not "let you." The teacher: "Being a detective lets you."
correct_examples: Being a detective lets you experience a different life.
Living abroad lets you see the world differently.
context: Re-stating the pros of being a detective.

my_sentence: They help the wife get more money when she divorced with her husband.
correction: They help the wife get more money when she divorces her husband.
explanation: "Divorce" takes a direct object with no preposition: "divorce someone," not "divorce with someone." (assistant note)
correct_examples: She divorced her husband last year.
He decided to divorce his wife.
context: How detectives are useful in divorce cases.

my_sentence: They can help victory get enough refined. (intended: victims / compensation)
correction: They help victims get enough compensation.
explanation: "Victims" was pronounced so it sounded like "victory/Victor," and the teacher repeatedly heard "Victor." Watch the vowel and ending: VIC-tims /ˈvɪktɪmz/ vs. VIC-tory /ˈvɪktəri/.
correct_examples: Detectives help victims get compensation.
The victims of the accident were taken to hospital.
context: How detectives help people.

my_sentence: They can through your actions to get some information.
correction: They can observe your actions to get some information.
explanation: "Through" is a preposition, not a verb — this is direct transfer from Chinese 通过. Use a real verb such as "observe," or restructure: "They can get information through/by observing your actions."
correct_examples: They can observe your actions to get information.
Detectives gather information by observing small details.
context: How detectives use observation skills.

my_sentence: They can observe your actions about your finger. Wiggle, wiggle finger.
correction: They can observe you wiggling your fingers.
explanation: After "observe someone," use the -ing form to describe the action observed: "observe you wiggling your fingers," not a bare noun phrase like "your finger wiggle."
correct_examples: The detective observed the man wiggling his fingers.
I noticed him tapping his foot nervously.
context: Examples of small details detectives notice.

my_sentence: I have thought that being a detective was cool during my childhood.
correction: I thought that being a detective was cool when I was a child.
explanation: A finished past period ("during my childhood") takes the simple past, not the present perfect. The teacher: "I thought — you don't have to say have."
correct_examples: I thought detectives were cool when I was young.
As a child, I believed policemen were heroes.
context: Whether he thought detectives were cool as a child.

my_sentence: One of my classmates become the policeman many years ago.
correction: One of my classmates became a police officer many years ago.
explanation: "Many years ago" requires the past tense "became." Also use the indefinite article for a profession: "became a police officer," not "the policeman."
correct_examples: One of my classmates became a police officer many years ago.
She became a teacher after college.
context: Talking about a classmate who joined the police.

my_sentence: He is not an ordinary policeman. It is called a traffic policeman.
correction: He is a traffic enforcer. / He is a traffic policeman.
explanation: Use "he" for a person, not "it," and "is," not "is called," for stating someone's job. The teacher also offered the term "traffic enforcer."
correct_examples: He is a traffic enforcer.
He works as a traffic policeman.
context: Describing the classmate's job.

my_sentence: During the holiday it was very busy and he need to make sure the traffic well.
correction: During the holidays he was very busy and needed to keep the traffic flowing.
explanation: Say "during the holidays" (plural), and keep the past tense throughout: "needed to," not "need to." "Make sure the traffic well" is not English — use "keep the traffic flowing" or "direct the traffic."
correct_examples: During the holidays he needed to direct the traffic.
He was busy keeping the traffic flowing.
context: The classmate's work during holidays.

my_sentence: I didn't communicate with him, so I'm not sure or was he nowadays.
correction: I'm not sure how he is nowadays.
explanation: In an embedded question, keep statement word order: "how he is," not "how is he." The question word here is "how," not "or."
correct_examples: I'm not sure how he is nowadays.
I don't know where she lives now.
context: Losing touch with the classmate after moving.

my_sentence: You need to be stand on the street.
correction: You need to stand on the street.
explanation: "Stand" is an active verb here, so no "be": "need to stand," not "need to be stand." Only use "be" with an adjective or passive participle.
correct_examples: You need to stand on the street to direct traffic.
He has to stand outside for hours.
context: Why a traffic officer's job is hard.

my_sentence: You may be hit by someone who drink a lot.
correction: You may be hit by someone who drinks a lot.
explanation: "Someone" is singular, so the relative-clause verb takes -s: "someone who drinks."
correct_examples: He was hit by someone who drinks a lot.
Anyone who breaks the rule will be punished.
context: Dangers of standing in the street as a traffic officer.

my_sentence: I don't think so, if you are not proposal and does that.
correction: If you do not do it on purpose... / If you do not have a purpose...
explanation: "Proposal" (a suggestion/plan) was the wrong word — the intended meaning is intent: "do something on purpose" or "intentionally." The teacher recast it as "If you do not have a purpose."
correct_examples: If you didn't do it on purpose, you won't go to prison.
He didn't hit anyone intentionally.
context: Whether a driver who hits a traffic officer goes to jail.

my_sentence: If you don't hurt someone serious...
correction: If you don't hurt someone seriously...
explanation: To modify the verb "hurt," use the adverb "seriously," not the adjective "serious."
correct_examples: If you don't injure anyone seriously, you just pay a fine.
He was seriously hurt in the accident.
context: Consequences of traffic accidents.

my_sentence: They don't need to walk over. (intended: work overtime)
correction: They don't need to work overtime.
explanation: "Work" /wɜːrk/ was pronounced like "walk" /wɔːk/, so the teacher heard "walk over." Contrast the vowels: work-walk, and say the full phrase "work overtime."
correct_examples: Government employees don't need to work overtime.
I had to work overtime last week.
context: Working hours of police officers in China.

my_sentence: We are so close to the shopping mall so I can eat dinner at twelve o'clock.
correction: We can eat dinner there at twelve o'clock.
explanation: Since the plan involves the whole family, the subject is "we," and the place already mentioned is referred to with "there": "We can eat dinner there." The teacher modeled "We can eat dinner."
correct_examples: We can eat dinner there at twelve and come back by one.
The mall is close, so we can have lunch there.
context: Weekend plan to shop and eat at the mall with family.

my_sentence: If we meet something that we can't fix, we will recruit some detectives to help us.
correction: If we run into something we can't fix, we will hire a detective to help us.
explanation: "Recruit" means to enlist someone as an employee or member; a client engaging a professional "hires" them. Also "run into / come across a problem" is more natural than "meet something." (assistant note)
correct_examples: They hired a detective to find the truth.
If we run into a legal problem, we hire an expert.
context: Why ordinary people turn to detectives.
